controls.name: Vulnerabilities scanned and remediated
controls.description: Host-based vulnerability scans are performed at least quarterly on all external-facing systems. Critical and high vulnerabilities are tracked to remediation. domain: VULNERABILITY_PATCH_MANAGEMENT type: detective framework_requirement: "CC 2.1,CC 4.1,CC 7.1,CC 7.2,CC 7.4,CC 8.1" framework_mappings: nist_csf_2_0:

controls.name: Production inventory maintained
controls.description:
The Production Inventory Maintained control is designed to ensure that all production assets, including hardware, software, and data, are accurately tracked and managed throughout their lifecycle. This control is crucial for mitigating risks associated with unauthorized access, data breaches, and compliance failures, as it aligns with the SOC 2 trust service principles of security and availability. By maintaining a comprehensive inventory, organizations can quickly identify vulnerabilities, ensure proper asset management, and demonstrate compliance during audits.

To implement this control effectively, organizations should establish a centralized inventory management system that records all production assets, including their configurations, locations, and ownership. Regular audits should be conducted to verify the accuracy of the inventory, and automated tools can be employed to streamline the tracking process. Key components to consider include asset tagging, regular updates to the inventory as new assets are added or removed, and integration with other security tools to enhance visibility.

Measurement and monitoring of this control can be achieved by tracking metrics such as the percentage of assets accurately recorded in the inventory, the frequency of inventory audits, and the time taken to reconcile discrepancies. Organizations should also maintain logs of inventory changes and audit results to provide evidence of compliance and effective asset management. Adhering to industry best practices, such as those outlined by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), can further strengthen the effectiveness of this control and support ongoing SOC 2 compliance efforts.

controls.name: SOC 2 - System Description
controls.description:
The SOC 2 - System Description control serves to document and articulate the operational framework of your organization’s systems, including the services provided and the internal controls implemented to meet the Trust Services Criteria. This control is crucial for ensuring transparency and accountability, as it helps stakeholders understand how data is managed and protected, thereby mitigating risks associated with unauthorized access and data breaches. A well-crafted system description not only supports compliance with SOC 2 requirements but also enhances trust with clients and partners by demonstrating a commitment to data security and privacy.

To implement this control effectively, organizations should begin by compiling a comprehensive overview of their systems, including architecture diagrams, data flow maps, and descriptions of key processes. It is essential to detail the specific controls in place, such as access controls, encryption methods, and incident response protocols, while also explaining how these controls align with the Trust Services Criteria. Regular updates to the system description should be scheduled to reflect any changes in the IT environment or operational processes, ensuring that the documentation remains accurate and relevant.

Key components to consider include the identification of all system components, the delineation of roles and responsibilities, and the integration of change management practices to ensure that any modifications to the system are documented and assessed for risk. Measurement and monitoring can be achieved through regular reviews of the system description against actual practices, audits of compliance with documented controls, and tracking metrics such as the number of changes made and the time taken to update documentation. Adhering to industry best practices, such as those outlined by Thoropass, will further strengthen the effectiveness of this control by ensuring that change management processes are robust and aligned with SOC 2 criteria.

risks.name: Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
risks.description:
In a scenario where vulnerability and system monitoring procedures are inadequately established due to human error or misunderstanding of company requirements, an organization could face a significant breach of its operational security. For example, an employee tasked with monitoring system vulnerabilities may misinterpret the guidelines, leading to critical patches not being applied in a timely manner. This oversight could allow cybercriminals to exploit known vulnerabilities, gaining unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. Attack vectors may include phishing attacks that trick employees into providing access credentials or exploiting unpatched software vulnerabilities that are publicly known.

The business impact of such a breach can be severe, resulting in financial losses, reputational damage, and potential legal ramifications, especially if the organization is subject to compliance frameworks like SOC 2, which emphasizes the importance of effective vulnerability management. Non-compliance could lead to loss of client trust and the inability to secure future contracts, as clients increasingly demand transparency and assurance regarding data security practices. Stakeholders affected would include IT staff responsible for security, the broader employee base, clients whose data may be compromised, and executive leadership facing the fallout from the breach.

Real-world examples highlight the importance of robust vulnerability management; for instance, the 2021 Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack was partly attributed to inadequate patch management, which allowed attackers to exploit a known vulnerability. Similarly, the Equifax breach in 2017 occurred when a critical patch was not applied, exposing sensitive personal information of millions. These incidents underscore the necessity of clear communication and training regarding vulnerability management procedures to mitigate the risk of human error leading to significant security breaches.
risks.trigger: human error and/or misunderstanding of company requirements

dt_semantic_questions.semantic_search: What SOC2 risks and incident scenarios are associated with vulnerability management and asset inventory (e.g., risks from unpatched critical vulnerabilities, unknown assets, stale asset data) and how are they described for SOC2 control mappings?
dt_semantic_questions.metrics_lookup: Which trend metrics in the 'asset_inventory' category (for SOC2) are available from Qualys that measure asset counts, asset ownership coverage, last-seen/staleness, and mappings between assets and vulnerabilities over time?
dt_semantic_questions.mdl_lookup: Retrieve GoldStandardTable records for project 'cve_data' that contain canonical CVE identifiers, severity baselines, vendor/patch advisories, and canonical mapping keys usable for SOC2 metric traceability.

dt_mdl_lookup_queries.vulnerability_management: What Qualys and Snyk tables/schemas contain vulnerability detection and findings data for SOC2 vulnerability_management, including fields: vulnerability_id/CVE, severity, first_seen, last_seen, state/status, patch_state, host/asset_id, and owner mappings? Retrieve GoldStandardTable records for project 'cve_data' that contain canonical CVE identifiers, severity baselines, vendor/patch advisories, and canonical mapping keys usable for SOC2 metric traceability.
dt_mdl_lookup_queries.asset_inventory: What Qualys tables/schemas contain asset inventory data for SOC2 asset_inventory, including fields: asset_id, hostname, IP, owner, last_seen, asset_tags, asset_type, and mappings to vulnerability identifiers?

groups.goal: Monitor overall compliance status against framework controls

kpis.name: Vulnerability Acceptance Tracking
kpis.description: Track accepted (risk-accepted) vulnerabilities with expiration and severity for exception management.
kpis.natural_language_question: How many vulnerabilities are currently risk-accepted, and which acceptances expire in the next 30 days?

kpis.name: CVE Exploitability Metrics
kpis.description: Exploit maturity, EPSS, weaponized status for prioritization. Threat context.
kpis.natural_language_question: How many of our open vulnerabilities are known to be exploited in the wild, and what is their EPSS distribution?

kpis.name: Vulnerability Fix Backlog by Team
kpis.description: Counts and ages unremediated vulnerability findings by responsible engineering or operations teams to track backlog and overdue remediation. Helps prioritize remediation resource allocation and identifies teams with persistent remediation slippage.
kpis.natural_language_question: Which teams have the largest backlog of unresolved vulnerabilities and how has each team's backlog trended over the last 90 days?

kpis.name: Vulnerabilities by Asset Criticality
kpis.description: Vulnerability count and severity weighted by asset criticality. Blast radius and impact.
kpis.natural_language_question: How many critical vulnerabilities exist on our most critical assets, and how has that changed?

kpis.name: Authenticated vs unauthenticated scan ratio
kpis.description: Compares counts and coverage of scans by TRACKING_METHOD (authenticated vs unauthenticated) per ASSET_ID/OS to identify gaps where authenticated scanning should be increased for deeper coverage.
kpis.natural_language_question: How many asset scans are authenticated versus unauthenticated and which asset groups lack authenticated coverage?

groups.goal: Measure how well individual controls mitigate their target risks
Quantify and track risk exposure across the environment

outputs.reasoning: Query seeks to identify metrics for tracking trends in vulnerability management and asset management compliance, which requires time-series aggregation and references resolved metrics that involve trend analysis.

resolved_metrics.name: Vulnerabilities by Asset Criticality
resolved_metrics.description: Vulnerability count and severity weighted by asset criticality. Blast radius and impact.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: How many critical vulnerabilities exist on our most critical assets, and how has that changed?

resolved_metrics.name: Vulnerability Acceptance Tracking
resolved_metrics.description: Track accepted (risk-accepted) vulnerabilities with expiration and severity for exception management.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: How many vulnerabilities are currently risk-accepted, and which acceptances expire in the next 30 days?

resolved_metrics.name: Vulnerability Fix Backlog by Team
resolved_metrics.description: Counts and ages unremediated vulnerability findings by responsible engineering or operations teams to track backlog and overdue remediation. Helps prioritize remediation resource allocation and identifies teams with persistent remediation slippage.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: Which teams have the largest backlog of unresolved vulnerabilities and how has each team's backlog trended over the last 90 days?

resolved_metrics.name: CVE Exploitability Metrics
resolved_metrics.description: Exploit maturity, EPSS, weaponized status for prioritization. Threat context.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: How many of our open vulnerabilities are known to be exploited in the wild, and what is their EPSS distribution?

resolved_metrics.name: Asset Software Licensing Compliance
resolved_metrics.description: Measures installed software against licensed entitlements by site and department to quantify compliance and potential cost exposure. Identifies unlicensed installations, vendor-level noncompliance, and high-risk locations for procurement or audit action.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: What percentage of installed software is covered by valid licenses by vendor and site, and where are the largest concentrations of unlicensed software?

resolved_metrics.name: Asset Agent Coverage
resolved_metrics.description: Assets with agent presence (Balbix, Sentinel, etc.). Coverage gaps and agent health.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: What percentage of our assets have an active agent, and which sites have the most coverage gaps?

resolved_metrics.name: Software Inventory Count
resolved_metrics.description: Installed software per asset (CPE, vendor, product, version). SBOM and patch coverage.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: How many unique software products are installed across our estate, and what percentage have available but uninstalled patches?

resolved_metrics.name: Misconfiguration Count by Category
resolved_metrics.description: Misconfigurations per asset by category and severity. Config drift and compliance.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: How many misconfigurations do we have by category, and which assets have the most critical misconfigs?

resolved_metrics.name: Software Patch Gap
resolved_metrics.description: Gap between latest available patch and installed patch. Patch lag and exposure.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: What is our average patch lag for critical software, and which products have the largest gap?

resolved_metrics.name: Asset Open Ports Summary
resolved_metrics.description: Open ports per asset with service and traffic visibility. Attack surface and exposure.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: Which assets have the most open ports, and are there any newly observed ports in the last 7 days?

resolved_metrics.name: Authenticated vs unauthenticated scan ratio
resolved_metrics.description: Compares counts and coverage of scans by TRACKING_METHOD (authenticated vs unauthenticated) per ASSET_ID/OS to identify gaps where authenticated scanning should be increased for deeper coverage.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: How many asset scans are authenticated versus unauthenticated and which asset groups lack authenticated coverage?

resolved_metrics.name: CVE patch adoption latency
resolved_metrics.description: Measures time from CVE publication (or detection) to remediation for instances where a patch/fix_version exists, indicating how quickly patches are applied. Shorter adoption latency reduces risk exposure even when fixes are available.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: For CVEs that have patches available, how long does it take on average for teams to apply them and which assets lag?

resolved_metrics.name: CVE patch availability rate
resolved_metrics.description: Tracks the proportion of vulnerability instances that have a documented fix_version or patch entry to gauge remediation options availability. Knowing which instances lack patches helps prioritize workaround or mitigation actions.
trends: count of patchless instances over time
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: What portion of our vulnerability instances have an available patch or documented fix version, and which CVEs remain without fixes?

resolved_metrics.name: Asset Inventory Count
resolved_metrics.description: Total count of assets (devices) by type, platform, site, and location. Coverage and inventory health.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: How many assets do we have by platform and region, and how has our cloud asset count grown in the last quarter?

resolved_metrics.name: Scheduled scan coverage & freshness
resolved_metrics.description: Measures the proportion of assets covered by scheduled scans and the time since last scheduled scan, showing how current scan scheduling keeps asset coverage fresh. This helps identify assets that are falling out of scanning cadence and therefore at higher undetected risk.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: Which critical asset groups have not been covered by a scheduled scan in the past 30 days and what scan schedules are responsible?

resolved_metrics.name: Vulnerability fix coverage by CVE and version
resolved_metrics.description: Tracks which vulnerabilities have an identified fixed_version or remediation guidance and the coverage of fixes across affected items. Important to know remediation availability and coverage to plan rollouts.
trends: percentage fixed by severity over time
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: What proportion of reported vulnerabilities have an available fix or documented remediation, and how does that vary by severity?

resolved_metrics.name: Asset centrality in attack paths
resolved_metrics.description: Counts how often an asset appears as a source or target across attack paths to identify hubs that concentrate risk and could amplify compromise. Central assets are good candidates for additional hardening or monitoring.
resolved_metrics.natural_language_question: Which assets act as central hubs in attack paths and what percentage of total paths do the top 10 assets account for?

field_instructions.name: is_vulnerability_fixed
field_instructions.description: Indicates whether a vulnerability has been fixed.

field_instructions.name: vulnerability_severity
field_instructions.description: Severity level of the vulnerability based on CVSS score.
field_instructions.calculation_basis: CASE WHEN v1.issue.cvssScore >= 9.0 THEN 'critical' WHEN v1.issue.cvssScore >= 7.0 THEN 'high' WHEN v1.issue.cvssScore >= 4.0 THEN 'medium' ELSE 'low' END

field_instructions.name: days_since_last_scan
field_instructions.description: Number of days since the last vulnerability scan was performed.

metric_instructions.name: critical_vulns_on_critical_assets
metric_instructions.description: Count of critical vulnerabilities on critical assets.
metric_instructions.measure: COUNT(*) WHERE vulnerability_severity = 'critical' AND is_vulnerability_fixed = FALSE
metric_instructions.filters: Only critical vulnerabilities that are not fixed

metric_instructions.name: weighted_vuln_count
metric_instructions.description: Count of vulnerabilities weighted by asset criticality.

metric_instructions.name: accepted_vuln_count
metric_instructions.description: Count of vulnerabilities that have been accepted as risks.

metric_instructions.name: expiring_acceptances
metric_instructions.description: Count of accepted vulnerabilities that will expire in the next 30 days.
metric_instructions.measure: COUNT(*) WHERE expiration_date BETWEEN CURRENT_DATE AND CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL '30 days'
metric_instructions.filters: Only accepted vulnerabilities with expiration dates

metric_instructions.name: avg_days_open
metric_instructions.description: Average number of days vulnerabilities have been open.
metric_instructions.measure: AVG(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - created_at))/86400) WHERE is_vulnerability_fixed = FALSE

calculation_plan.reasoning: Mapped user intent regarding SOC2 vulnerability management metrics to specific KPIs and trends. Field instructions were created for determining if vulnerabilities are fixed, their severity, and days since last scan. Metric instructions were developed to track critical vulnerabilities on critical assets, weighted vulnerability counts, accepted vulnerabilities, and trends over time using available columns from the schemas.

dt_retrieved_controls.name: Production inventory maintained
dt_retrieved_controls.description:
The Production Inventory Maintained control is designed to ensure that all production assets, including hardware, software, and data, are accurately tracked and managed throughout their lifecycle. This control is crucial for mitigating risks associated with unauthorized access, data breaches, and compliance failures, as it aligns with the SOC 2 trust service principles of security and availability. By maintaining a comprehensive inventory, organizations can quickly identify vulnerabilities, ensure proper asset management, and demonstrate compliance during audits.

To implement this control effectively, organizations should establish a centralized inventory management system that records all production assets, including their configurations, locations, and ownership. Regular audits should be conducted to verify the accuracy of the inventory, and automated tools can be employed to streamline the tracking process. Key components to consider include asset tagging, regular updates to the inventory as new assets are added or removed, and integration with other security tools to enhance visibility.

Measurement and monitoring of this control can be achieved by tracking metrics such as the percentage of assets accurately recorded in the inventory, the frequency of inventory audits, and the time taken to reconcile discrepancies. Organizations should also maintain logs of inventory changes and audit results to provide evidence of compliance and effective asset management. Adhering to industry best practices, such as those outlined by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), can further strengthen the effectiveness of this control and support ongoing SOC 2 compliance efforts.

dt_retrieved_controls.name: Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
SOC 2 - System Description
dt_retrieved_controls.description:
The SOC 2 - System Description control serves to document and articulate the operational framework of your organization’s systems, including the services provided and the internal controls implemented to meet the Trust Services Criteria. This control is crucial for ensuring transparency and accountability, as it helps stakeholders understand how data is managed and protected, thereby mitigating risks associated with unauthorized access and data breaches. A well-crafted system description not only supports compliance with SOC 2 requirements but also enhances trust with clients and partners by demonstrating a commitment to data security and privacy.

To implement this control effectively, organizations should begin by compiling a comprehensive overview of their systems, including architecture diagrams, data flow maps, and descriptions of key processes. It is essential to detail the specific controls in place, such as access controls, encryption methods, and incident response protocols, while also explaining how these controls align with the Trust Services Criteria. Regular updates to the system description should be scheduled to reflect any changes in the IT environment or operational processes, ensuring that the documentation remains accurate and relevant.

Key components to consider include the identification of all system components, the delineation of roles and responsibilities, and the integration of change management practices to ensure that any modifications to the system are documented and assessed for risk. Measurement and monitoring can be achieved through regular reviews of the system description against actual practices, audits of compliance with documented controls, and tracking metrics such as the number of changes made and the time taken to update documentation. Adhering to industry best practices, such as those outlined by Thoropass, will further strengthen the effectiveness of this control by ensuring that change management processes are robust and aligned with SOC 2 criteria.

dt_retrieved_risks.name: Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
dt_retrieved_risks.description:
In a scenario where vulnerability and system monitoring procedures are inadequately established due to human error or misunderstanding of company requirements, an organization could face a significant breach of its operational security. For example, an employee tasked with monitoring system vulnerabilities may misinterpret the guidelines, leading to critical patches not being applied in a timely manner. This oversight could allow cybercriminals to exploit known vulnerabilities, gaining unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. Attack vectors may include phishing attacks that trick employees into providing access credentials or exploiting unpatched software vulnerabilities that are publicly known.

The business impact of such a breach can be severe, resulting in financial losses, reputational damage, and potential legal ramifications, especially if the organization is subject to compliance frameworks like SOC 2, which emphasizes the importance of effective vulnerability management. Non-compliance could lead to loss of client trust and the inability to secure future contracts, as clients increasingly demand transparency and assurance regarding data security practices. Stakeholders affected would include IT staff responsible for security, the broader employee base, clients whose data may be compromised, and executive leadership facing the fallout from the breach.

Real-world examples highlight the importance of robust vulnerability management; for instance, the 2021 Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack was partly attributed to inadequate patch management, which allowed attackers to exploit a known vulnerability. Similarly, the Equifax breach in 2017 occurred when a critical patch was not applied, exposing sensitive personal information of millions. These incidents underscore the necessity of clear communication and training regarding vulnerability management procedures to mitigate the risk of human error leading to significant security breaches.
dt_retrieved_risks.trigger: human error and/or misunderstanding of company requirements

Critical and high vulnerabilities are tracked to remediation. domain: VULNERABILITY_PATCH_MANAGEMENT type: detective framework_requirement: "CC 2.1,CC 4.1,CC 7.1,CC 7.2,CC 7.4,CC 8.1" framework_mappings: nist_csf_2_0:

controls.name: Production inventory maintained
controls.description:
The Production Inventory Maintained control is designed to ensure that all production assets, including hardware, software, and data, are accurately tracked and managed throughout their lifecycle. This control is crucial for mitigating risks associated with unauthorized access, data breaches, and compliance failures, as it aligns with the SOC 2 trust service principles of security and availability. By maintaining a comprehensive inventory, organizations can quickly identify vulnerabilities, ensure proper asset management, and demonstrate compliance during audits.

To implement this control effectively, organizations should establish a centralized inventory management system that records all production assets, including their configurations, locations, and ownership. Regular audits should be conducted to verify the accuracy of the inventory, and automated tools can be employed to streamline the tracking process. Key components to consider include asset tagging, regular updates to the inventory as new assets are added or removed, and integration with other security tools to enhance visibility.

Measurement and monitoring of this control can be achieved by tracking metrics such as the percentage of assets accurately recorded in the inventory, the frequency of inventory audits, and the time taken to reconcile discrepancies. Organizations should also maintain logs of inventory changes and audit results to provide evidence of compliance and effective asset management. Adhering to industry best practices, such as those outlined by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), can further strengthen the effectiveness of this control and support ongoing SOC 2 compliance efforts.

controls.name: SOC 2 - System Description
controls.description:
The SOC 2 - System Description control serves to document and articulate the operational framework of your organization’s systems, including the services provided and the internal controls implemented to meet the Trust Services Criteria. This control is crucial for ensuring transparency and accountability, as it helps stakeholders understand how data is managed and protected, thereby mitigating risks associated with unauthorized access and data breaches. A well-crafted system description not only supports compliance with SOC 2 requirements but also enhances trust with clients and partners by demonstrating a commitment to data security and privacy.

To implement this control effectively, organizations should begin by compiling a comprehensive overview of their systems, including architecture diagrams, data flow maps, and descriptions of key processes. It is essential to detail the specific controls in place, such as access controls, encryption methods, and incident response protocols, while also explaining how these controls align with the Trust Services Criteria. Regular updates to the system description should be scheduled to reflect any changes in the IT environment or operational processes, ensuring that the documentation remains accurate and relevant.

Key components to consider include the identification of all system components, the delineation of roles and responsibilities, and the integration of change management practices to ensure that any modifications to the system are documented and assessed for risk. Measurement and monitoring can be achieved through regular reviews of the system description against actual practices, audits of compliance with documented controls, and tracking metrics such as the number of changes made and the time taken to update documentation. Adhering to industry best practices, such as those outlined by Thoropass, will further strengthen the effectiveness of this control by ensuring that change management processes are robust and aligned with SOC 2 criteria.

risks.name: Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
Vulnerability and system monitoring procedures established
risks.description:
In a scenario where vulnerability and system monitoring procedures are inadequately established due to human error or misunderstanding of company requirements, an organization could face a significant breach of its operational security. For example, an employee tasked with monitoring system vulnerabilities may misinterpret the guidelines, leading to critical patches not being applied in a timely manner. This oversight could allow cybercriminals to exploit known vulnerabilities, gaining unauthorized access to sensitive data or systems. Attack vectors may include phishing attacks that trick employees into providing access credentials or exploiting unpatched software vulnerabilities that are publicly known.

The business impact of such a breach can be severe, resulting in financial losses, reputational damage, and potential legal ramifications, especially if the organization is subject to compliance frameworks like SOC 2, which emphasizes the importance of effective vulnerability management. Non-compliance could lead to loss of client trust and the inability to secure future contracts, as clients increasingly demand transparency and assurance regarding data security practices. Stakeholders affected would include IT staff responsible for security, the broader employee base, clients whose data may be compromised, and executive leadership facing the fallout from the breach.

Real-world examples highlight the importance of robust vulnerability management; for instance, the 2021 Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack was partly attributed to inadequate patch management, which allowed attackers to exploit a known vulnerability. Similarly, the Equifax breach in 2017 occurred when a critical patch was not applied, exposing sensitive personal information of millions. These incidents underscore the necessity of clear communication and training regarding vulnerability management procedures to mitigate the risk of human error leading to significant security breaches.
risks.trigger: human error and/or misunderstanding of company requirements

scored_metrics.name: Vulnerabilities by Asset Criticality
scored_metrics.description: Vulnerability count and severity weighted by asset criticality. Blast radius and impact.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: How many critical vulnerabilities exist on our most critical assets, and how has that changed?

scored_metrics.name: Vulnerability Acceptance Tracking
scored_metrics.description: Track accepted (risk-accepted) vulnerabilities with expiration and severity for exception management.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: How many vulnerabilities are currently risk-accepted, and which acceptances expire in the next 30 days?

scored_metrics.name: Vulnerability Fix Backlog by Team
scored_metrics.description: Counts and ages unremediated vulnerability findings by responsible engineering or operations teams to track backlog and overdue remediation. Helps prioritize remediation resource allocation and identifies teams with persistent remediation slippage.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: Which teams have the largest backlog of unresolved vulnerabilities and how has each team's backlog trended over the last 90 days?

scored_metrics.name: CVE Exploitability Metrics
scored_metrics.description: Exploit maturity, EPSS, weaponized status for prioritization. Threat context.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: How many of our open vulnerabilities are known to be exploited in the wild, and what is their EPSS distribution?

scored_metrics.name: Asset Software Licensing Compliance
scored_metrics.description: Measures installed software against licensed entitlements by site and department to quantify compliance and potential cost exposure. Identifies unlicensed installations, vendor-level noncompliance, and high-risk locations for procurement or audit action.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: What percentage of installed software is covered by valid licenses by vendor and site, and where are the largest concentrations of unlicensed software?

scored_metrics.name: Asset Agent Coverage
scored_metrics.description: Assets with agent presence (Balbix, Sentinel, etc.). Coverage gaps and agent health.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: What percentage of our assets have an active agent, and which sites have the most coverage gaps?

scored_metrics.name: Software Inventory Count
scored_metrics.description: Installed software per asset (CPE, vendor, product, version). SBOM and patch coverage.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: How many unique software products are installed across our estate, and what percentage have available but uninstalled patches?

scored_metrics.name: Misconfiguration Count by Category
scored_metrics.description: Misconfigurations per asset by category and severity. Config drift and compliance.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: How many misconfigurations do we have by category, and which assets have the most critical misconfigs?

scored_metrics.name: Software Patch Gap
scored_metrics.description: Gap between latest available patch and installed patch. Patch lag and exposure.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: What is our average patch lag for critical software, and which products have the largest gap?

scored_metrics.name: Asset Open Ports Summary
scored_metrics.description: Open ports per asset with service and traffic visibility. Attack surface and exposure.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: Which assets have the most open ports, and are there any newly observed ports in the last 7 days?

scored_metrics.name: Authenticated vs unauthenticated scan ratio
scored_metrics.description: Compares counts and coverage of scans by TRACKING_METHOD (authenticated vs unauthenticated) per ASSET_ID/OS to identify gaps where authenticated scanning should be increased for deeper coverage.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: How many asset scans are authenticated versus unauthenticated and which asset groups lack authenticated coverage?

scored_metrics.name: CVE patch adoption latency
scored_metrics.description: Measures time from CVE publication (or detection) to remediation for instances where a patch/fix_version exists, indicating how quickly patches are applied. Shorter adoption latency reduces risk exposure even when fixes are available.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: For CVEs that have patches available, how long does it take on average for teams to apply them and which assets lag?

scored_metrics.name: CVE patch availability rate
scored_metrics.description: Tracks the proportion of vulnerability instances that have a documented fix_version or patch entry to gauge remediation options availability. Knowing which instances lack patches helps prioritize workaround or mitigation actions.
trends: count of patchless instances over time
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: What portion of our vulnerability instances have an available patch or documented fix version, and which CVEs remain without fixes?

scored_metrics.name: Asset Inventory Count
scored_metrics.description: Total count of assets (devices) by type, platform, site, and location. Coverage and inventory health.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: How many assets do we have by platform and region, and how has our cloud asset count grown in the last quarter?

scored_metrics.name: Scheduled scan coverage & freshness
scored_metrics.description: Measures the proportion of assets covered by scheduled scans and the time since last scheduled scan, showing how current scan scheduling keeps asset coverage fresh. This helps identify assets that are falling out of scanning cadence and therefore at higher undetected risk.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: Which critical asset groups have not been covered by a scheduled scan in the past 30 days and what scan schedules are responsible?

scored_metrics.name: Vulnerability fix coverage by CVE and version
scored_metrics.description: Tracks which vulnerabilities have an identified fixed_version or remediation guidance and the coverage of fixes across affected items. Important to know remediation availability and coverage to plan rollouts.
trends: percentage fixed by severity over time
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: What proportion of reported vulnerabilities have an available fix or documented remediation, and how does that vary by severity?

scored_metrics.name: Asset centrality in attack paths
scored_metrics.description: Counts how often an asset appears as a source or target across attack paths to identify hubs that concentrate risk and could amplify compromise. Central assets are good candidates for additional hardening or monitoring.
scored_metrics.natural_language_question: Which assets act as central hubs in attack paths and what percentage of total paths do the top 10 assets account for?

Executive Summary & Compliance Mapping.overview: This playbook bundles metric definitions, medallion data architecture guidance, field-level calculations, evidence mapping and implementation steps to meet SOC2 expectations for vulnerability and asset management. Metrics were derived from Qualys (host detections, hosts, applications) and Snyk (code/dependency issues). SIEM rule coverage is missing (validation failed).

compliance_mapping_summary.SOC2_alignment: Supports SOC2 Security and Availability Trust Services Criteria by providing continuous detection, tracking, and remediation evidence for vulnerabilities (identification, prioritization, remediation tracking, trending).
example_controls_supported: Scan cadence and detection (evidence of regular scanning & discovery)

compliance_mapping_summary.SOC2_alignment: Supports evidence of maintained, authoritative asset inventory and asset coverage (agent and scanning coverage), which is required to ensure systems in-scope are known and monitored.
example_controls_supported: Inventory of authorized systems and applications
Agent/coverage rate to demonstrate scan presence across in-scope systems

top_risks_and_gaps: No SIEM rules present — SIEM evidence for alerting/monitoring is missing and must be created or mapped to satisfy incident detection requirements.
Gold-level KPI tables are recommended but not yet available (gold_available=false for recommended gold tables).
Some additional valuable metrics require sources not in scope (e.g., patch metadata, vulnerability acceptance trackers, other EDR/VMS platforms). See Gap Notes section.

primary_focus: Critical vuln counts and trends (per host, per OS)
Open vulnerability backlog and age (days open)
Snyk/Code vulnerability counts and language-level trends
Asset inventory, application inventory, and agent coverage

calculation_plan_high_level: Filter severity=CRITICAL and status=open (use normalized status values).
Join qualys_hosts to get host attributes (ip, os, canonical_id).
Daily snapshot per host (derive date from last_found_datetime).
Produce rolling 30-day totals and compare to prior 30-day window.

principles: Bronze: Ingest raw Qualys and Snyk export tables (API dumps), retaining original timestamps, raw payloads and native IDs.
Silver: Normalized tables (qualys_host_detections, qualys_hosts, qualys_applications, snyk_issues) with consistent canonical IDs (host_id, asset_id, repo_id) and normalized status/severity fields.
Gold: KPI-grade aggregates at daily or weekly grain (gold_* tables). Gold tables are the authoritative reporting layer for SOC2 evidence and dashboards.

general_guidance: Normalize severity and status fields across Qualys and Snyk. For Qualys map text severities to standardized bands (CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW). For Snyk use Snyk severity and CVSS where available.
All snapshots must use a consistent timezone (UTC recommended) and consistent snapshot_date/time rounding (e.g., DATE(last_found_datetime) for daily grain).
De-duplicate findings by canonical finding_id (use Qualys finding_id or Snyk issue id) before counting.
When computing 'days open' prefer a first_seen column; if only last_found_datetime exists, derive first_seen from earliest observed record for that finding.

example_sql_snippets.critical_vulns_per_host_daily_snapshot:
-- pseudo-SQL
SELECT
  DATE(d.last_found_datetime) AS snapshot_date,
  d.host_id,
  h.ip AS host_ip,
  h.os AS host_os,
  COUNT(DISTINCT d.finding_id) FILTER (WHERE d.severity = 'CRITICAL' AND d.status = 'OPEN') AS critical_vuln_count
FROM qualys_host_detections d
JOIN qualys_hosts h ON h.id = d.host_id
WHERE d.last_found_datetime >= DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE, INTERVAL 90 DAY)
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4;
example_sql_snippets.weekly_gold_aggregation_by_host:
-- pseudo-SQL
INSERT INTO gold_vulnerabilities_weekly_by_host (week_start, host_id, host_ip, host_os, critical_vuln_count)
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('week', snapshot_date) AS week_start,
  host_id,
  ANY_VALUE(host_ip) AS host_ip,
  ANY_VALUE(host_os) AS host_os,
  SUM(critical_vuln_count) AS critical_vuln_count
FROM silver_daily_vuln_snapshots
GROUP BY 1,2;
example_sql_snippets.open_vuln_backlog_daily_with_age:
-- pseudo-SQL
WITH first_seen AS (
  SELECT finding_id, host_id, MIN(last_found_datetime) AS first_seen_dt
  FROM qualys_host_detections
  GROUP BY 1,2
)
SELECT
  DATE(d.last_found_datetime) AS snapshot_date,
  d.host_id,
  COUNT(DISTINCT d.finding_id) AS open_findings,
  AVG(DATEDIFF('day', fs.first_seen_dt, DATE(d.last_found_datetime))) AS avg_days_open
FROM qualys_host_detections d
JOIN first_seen fs USING (finding_id, host_id)
WHERE d.status = 'OPEN'
GROUP BY 1,2;
example_sql_snippets.snyk_daily_cvss_distribution:
-- pseudo-SQL
SELECT
  DATE(issue.created_at) AS snapshot_date,
  issue.language,
  SUM(CASE WHEN issue.cvss >= 9 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cvss_9_plus,
  SUM(CASE WHEN issue.cvss >= 7 AND issue.cvss < 9 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cvss_7_8_9,
  COUNT(*) AS total_issues
FROM snyk_issues issue
WHERE issue.org_id = 'YOUR_ORG'
GROUP BY 1,2;

{{ config(materialized='incremental', unique_key='connection_id, snapshot_date, host_id, qid', incremental_strategy='merge', on_schema_change='append_new_columns') }}

WITH source_data AS (
  SELECT
    hd.connection_id AS connection_id,
    date_trunc('day', CAST(hd.last_found_datetime AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE)) AS snapshot_date,
    hd.host_id AS host_id,
    h.ip AS ip,
    h.os AS os,
    hd.severity AS severity,
    CASE
      WHEN lower(CAST(hd.severity AS TEXT)) IN ('5','critical','crit') THEN 'critical'
      WHEN lower(CAST(hd.severity AS TEXT)) IN ('4','high') THEN 'high'
      WHEN lower(CAST(hd.severity AS TEXT)) IN ('3','medium','med') THEN 'medium'
      WHEN lower(CAST(hd.severity AS TEXT)) IN ('2','low') THEN 'low'
      WHEN lower(CAST(hd.severity AS TEXT)) IN ('1','info','informational') THEN 'info'
      ELSE lower(CAST(hd.severity AS TEXT))
    END AS severity_label,
    CASE
      WHEN lower(CAST(hd.severity AS TEXT)) IN ('5','critical','crit','4','high') THEN true
      ELSE false
    END AS is_critical,
    hd.status AS status,
    CASE
      WHEN lower(CAST(hd.status AS TEXT)) IN ('open','opened') THEN true
      ELSE false
    END AS is_open,
    CAST(hd.last_found_datetime AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS last_found_datetime,
    CAST( (CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - CAST(hd.last_found_datetime AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE)) / INTERVAL '1 day' AS INTEGER) AS days_open,
    hd.qid AS qid,
    app.name AS application_name,
    app.version AS application_version
  FROM
    {{ source('silver','qualys_host_detections') }} AS hd
  JOIN
    {{ source('silver','qualys_hosts') }} AS h
    ON hd.host_id = h.id
    AND hd.connection_id = h.connection_id
  LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('silver','qualys_applications') }} AS app
    ON hd.host_id = app.host_id
    AND hd.connection_id = app.connection_id
  WHERE
    hd.connection_id = {{ var('connection_id') }}
    {% if is_incremental() %}
      AND CAST(hd.last_found_datetime AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) >= (CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day')
    {% endif %}
)

SELECT
  sd.connection_id AS connection_id,
  sd.snapshot_date AS snapshot_date,
  sd.host_id AS host_id,
  sd.ip AS ip,
  sd.os AS os,
  sd.severity AS severity,
  sd.severity_label AS severity_label,
  sd.is_critical AS is_critical,
  sd.status AS status,
  sd.is_open AS is_open,
  sd.last_found_datetime AS last_found_datetime,
  sd.days_open AS days_open,
  sd.qid AS qid,
  sd.application_name AS application_name,
  sd.application_version AS application_version
FROM source_data AS sd
GROUP BY
  sd.connection_id,
  sd.snapshot_date,
  sd.host_id,
  sd.ip,
  sd.os,
  sd.severity,
  sd.severity_label,
  sd.is_critical,
  sd.status,
  sd.is_open,
  sd.last_found_datetime,
  sd.days_open,
  sd.qid,
  sd.application_name,
  sd.application_version;

{{ config(materialized='table', unique_key='connection_id, month_start, host_id, os', incremental_strategy='merge', on_schema_change='append_new_columns') }}

WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT
    src.connection_id AS connection_id,
    CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', src.week_start) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS month_start,

    SUM(src.critical_vuln_count_week) AS critical_vuln_count_month,

    CASE

      ELSE SUM(src.avg_days_open_week * src.open_vuln_count_week) / NULLIF(SUM(src.open_vuln_count_week), 0)
    END AS avg_days_open_month
  FROM {{ ref('gold_qualys_vulnerabilities_weekly_snapshot') }} AS src
  {% if is_incremental() %}

    AND src.connection_id = {{ var('connection_id') }}
  {% else %}
    WHERE src.connection_id = {{ var('connection_id') }}
  {% endif %}
  GROUP BY
    src.connection_id,
    CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', src.week_start) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE),
    src.host_id,
    src.os
)

SELECT

{{ config(materialized='incremental', unique_key='connection_id,organization_id,issue_package,publication_time', incremental_strategy='merge', on_schema_change='append_new_columns') }}

WITH source_data AS (
    SELECT
        src."connection_id" AS connection_id,
        src."organization_id" AS organization_id,
        -- normalize publication time to day-level snapshot (timestamp with time zone)
        CAST(date_trunc('day', CAST(src."v1.issue.publicationTime" AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE)) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS snapshot_date,
        src."v1.issue.package" AS issue_package,
        src."v1.issue.language" AS issue_language,
        -- cast CVSS score to numeric when it is a numeric-looking string
        CASE
            WHEN src."v1.issue.cvssScore" ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' THEN CAST(src."v1.issue.cvssScore" AS NUMERIC)
            ELSE NULL
        END AS cvss_score,
        -- derive high CVSS flag
        CASE
            WHEN src."v1.issue.cvssScore" ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$' AND CAST(src."v1.issue.cvssScore" AS NUMERIC) >= 7.0 THEN TRUE
            ELSE FALSE
        END AS is_high_cvss,
        CAST(src."v1.issue.publicationTime" AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS publication_time,
        src."v1.issue.semver.vulnerable" AS vulnerable_semver_ranges
    FROM {{ source('silver', 'snyk_issues') }} AS src
    {% if is_incremental() %}
    WHERE src."connection_id" = '{{ var("connection_id") }}'
    {% else %}
    WHERE src."connection_id" = '{{ var("connection_id") }}'
    {% endif %}
)

SELECT
    sd.connection_id,
    sd.organization_id,
    sd.snapshot_date,
    sd.issue_package,
    sd.issue_language,
    sd.cvss_score,
    sd.is_high_cvss,
    sd.publication_time,
    sd.vulnerable_semver_ranges
FROM source_data AS sd

{{ config(materialized='incremental', unique_key=['connection_id','week_start','issue_language','issue_package'], incremental_strategy='merge', on_schema_change='append_new_columns') }}

select

  ) AS cvss_distribution_week
from {{ ref('gold_snyk_issues_daily_snapshot') }} as daily
where daily.connection_id = {{ var('connection_id') }}
{% if is_incremental() %}
  -- incremental runs only process recent snapshots (by snapshot_date -> week_start)
  AND daily.snapshot_date >= (
    select coalesce(max(week_start), '1970-01-01'::timestamp) from {{ this }}
  )
{% endif %}
group by

  CAST(DATE_TRUNC('week', daily.snapshot_date) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE),
  daily.issue_language,
  daily.issue_package;

dt_generated_gold_model_sql.sql_query:
{{ config(materialized='table', unique_key='connection_id,month_start,issue_language,issue_package', incremental_strategy='merge', on_schema_change='append_new_columns') }}

WITH monthly_counts AS (
  SELECT
    ws.connection_id AS connection_id,
    CAST(date_trunc('month', ws.week_start) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS month_start,
    ws.issue_language AS issue_language,
    ws.issue_package AS issue_package,
    SUM(ws.count_high_cvss_week) AS monthly_high_cvss_count
  FROM {{ ref('gold_snyk_issues_weekly_snapshot') }} AS ws
  WHERE ws.connection_id = {{ var('connection_id') }}
  GROUP BY ws.connection_id, CAST(date_trunc('month', ws.week_start) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE), ws.issue_language, ws.issue_package
),
monthly_cvss_buckets AS (
  -- Unnest weekly JSON distributions into key/value rows, sum per month/language/package/key,
  -- then re-aggregate into a single JSON object per month/language/package
  SELECT
    sub.connection_id,
    sub.month_start,
    sub.issue_language,
    sub.issue_package,
    jsonb_object_agg(sub.cvss_key, sub.bucket_sum) AS monthly_cvss_distribution
  FROM (
    SELECT
      ws2.connection_id AS connection_id,
      CAST(date_trunc('month', ws2.week_start) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS month_start,
      ws2.issue_language AS issue_language,
      ws2.issue_package AS issue_package,
      pd.key AS cvss_key,
      SUM((pd.value)::bigint) AS bucket_sum
    FROM {{ ref('gold_snyk_issues_weekly_snapshot') }} AS ws2
    CROSS JOIN LATERAL jsonb_each_text(ws2.cvss_distribution_week) AS pd(key, value)
    WHERE ws2.connection_id = {{ var('connection_id') }}
    GROUP BY ws2.connection_id, CAST(date_trunc('month', ws2.week_start) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE), ws2.issue_language, ws2.issue_package, pd.key
  ) AS sub
  GROUP BY sub.connection_id, sub.month_start, sub.issue_language, sub.issue_package
)

SELECT
  mc.connection_id AS connection_id,
  mc.month_start AS month_start,
  mc.issue_language AS issue_language,
  mc.issue_package AS issue_package,
  mc.monthly_high_cvss_count AS monthly_high_cvss_count,
  COALESCE(mcv.monthly_cvss_distribution, '{}'::jsonb) AS monthly_cvss_distribution
FROM monthly_counts AS mc
LEFT JOIN monthly_cvss_buckets AS mcv
  ON mcv.connection_id = mc.connection_id
  AND mcv.month_start = mc.month_start
  AND mcv.issue_language = mc.issue_language
  AND mcv.issue_package = mc.issue_package

dt_generated_gold_model_sql.sql_query:
{{ config(materialized='incremental', unique_key='connection_id,week_start_date,host_id,name,version', incremental_strategy='merge', on_schema_change='append_new_columns') }}

WITH daily AS (
  SELECT
    d.connection_id AS connection_id,
    CAST(date_trunc('week', d.snapshot_date) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS week_start_date,
    d.host_id AS host_id,
    d.name AS name,
    d.version AS version,
    CAST(d.snapshot_date AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS snapshot_ts
  FROM {{ ref('gold_qualys_applications_daily_snapshot') }} AS d
  WHERE
    d.connection_id IS NOT NULL
    AND (
      NOT is_incremental()
      OR (is_incremental() AND d.snapshot_date >= date_trunc('week', current_date) - INTERVAL '7 days')
    )
),

-- Deduplicate to one row per host/software/version per week
weekly_presence AS (
  SELECT
    d.connection_id AS connection_id,
    d.week_start_date AS week_start_date,
    d.host_id AS host_id,
    d.name AS name,
    d.version AS version,
    MIN(d.snapshot_ts) AS first_seen_ts
  FROM daily AS d
  GROUP BY
    d.connection_id,
    d.week_start_date,
    d.host_id,
    d.name,
    d.version
)

SELECT
  w.connection_id,
  w.week_start_date,
  w.host_id,
  w.name,
  w.version,
  -- number of unique hosts in the connection/week that reported this name+version
  COUNT(DISTINCT w.host_id) OVER (PARTITION BY w.connection_id, w.week_start_date, w.name, w.version) AS unique_software_count,
  -- flag if there is no record for this host/name/version prior to the week_start_date -> new install within the last 7 days
  CASE
    WHEN NOT EXISTS (
      SELECT 1
      FROM {{ ref('gold_qualys_applications_daily_snapshot') }} AS e
      WHERE
        e.connection_id = w.connection_id
        AND e.host_id = w.host_id
        AND e.name = w.name
        AND e.version = w.version
        AND CAST(e.snapshot_date AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) < w.week_start_date
    ) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS new_installs_last_7_days_flag
FROM weekly_presence AS w

dt_generated_gold_model_sql.sql_query:
{{ config(materialized='incremental', unique_key='connection_id, week_start_date, organization_id', incremental_strategy='merge', on_schema_change='append_new_columns') }}

WITH hosts AS (
  SELECT
    h.connection_id AS connection_id,
    CAST(h.snapshot_date AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS week_start_date,
    h.organization_id AS organization_id,
    h.id AS host_id
  FROM {{ ref('gold_qualys_hosts_daily_snapshot') }} AS h
  WHERE 1 = 1
  {% if var('connection_id', none) is not none %}
    AND h.connection_id = {{ var('connection_id') }}
  {% endif %}
  {% if is_incremental() %}
    -- For incremental runs, only process snapshot_date on/after the latest week already in the target
    AND h.snapshot_date >= (
      SELECT COALESCE(MAX(week_start_date), '1970-01-01'::timestamp with time zone)
      FROM {{ this }}
    )
  {% endif %}
),

detections AS (
  SELECT
    d.connection_id AS connection_id,
    CAST(d.snapshot_date AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS week_start_date,
    d.id AS host_id,
    d.open_vulnerability_count AS open_vulnerability_count
  FROM {{ ref('gold_qualys_host_detections_weekly_snapshot') }} AS d
)

SELECT
  h.connection_id AS connection_id,
  h.week_start_date AS week_start_date,
  h.organization_id AS organization_id,
  COUNT(DISTINCT h.host_id) AS total_hosts,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN COALESCE(d.open_vulnerability_count, 0) > 0 THEN h.host_id END) AS hosts_with_recent_detections,
  (COUNT(DISTINCT h.host_id) - COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN COALESCE(d.open_vulnerability_count, 0) > 0 THEN h.host_id END)) AS hosts_without_recent_detections
FROM hosts AS h
LEFT JOIN detections AS d
  ON d.connection_id = h.connection_id
  AND d.week_start_date = h.week_start_date

dt_generated_gold_model_sql.name: gold_qualys_host_detections_monthly_snapshot
dt_generated_gold_model_sql.sql_query:
{{ config(materialized='table', unique_key='connection_id,month_start_date,host_id', incremental_strategy='merge', on_schema_change='append_new_columns') }}

WITH monthly_agg AS (
  SELECT
    ws.connection_id AS connection_id,
    CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', ws.week_start_date) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE) AS month_start_date,
    ws.host_id AS host_id,
    ws.os AS os,
    SUM(ws.open_vulnerability_count) AS monthly_open_vulnerability_count,
    CASE
      WHEN SUM(ws.open_vulnerability_count) = 0 THEN NULL

    END AS monthly_average_days_open
  FROM {{ ref('gold_qualys_host_detections_weekly_snapshot') }} AS ws
  WHERE ws.connection_id = {{ var('connection_id') }}
  GROUP BY
    ws.connection_id,
    CAST(DATE_TRUNC('month', ws.week_start_date) AS TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE),
    ws.host_id,
    ws.os
)

SELECT
  ma.connection_id,
  ma.month_start_date,
  ma.host_id,
  ma.os,
  ma.monthly_open_vulnerability_count,
  ma.monthly_average_days_open
FROM monthly_agg AS ma
ORDER BY ma.connection_id, ma.month_start_date, ma.host_id, ma.os;

goal_metric_definitions.name: Vulnerabilities by Asset Criticality
goal_metric_definitions.description: Vulnerability count and severity weighted by asset criticality. Blast radius and impact.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Vulnerability Acceptance Tracking
goal_metric_definitions.description: Track accepted (risk-accepted) vulnerabilities with expiration and severity for exception management.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Vulnerability Fix Backlog by Team
goal_metric_definitions.description: Counts and ages unremediated vulnerability findings by responsible engineering or operations teams to track backlog and overdue remediation. Helps prioritize remediation resource allocation and identifies teams with persistent remediation slippage.

goal_metric_definitions.name: CVE Exploitability Metrics
goal_metric_definitions.description: Exploit maturity, EPSS, weaponized status for prioritization. Threat context.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Vulnerabilities Per Asset
goal_metric_definitions.description: Measures the average number of vulnerabilities detected per active asset. This metric is crucial for risk assessment and prioritizing remediation efforts.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Asset Software Licensing Compliance
goal_metric_definitions.description: Measures installed software against licensed entitlements by site and department to quantify compliance and potential cost exposure. Identifies unlicensed installations, vendor-level noncompliance, and high-risk locations for procurement or audit action.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Asset Agent Coverage
goal_metric_definitions.description: Assets with agent presence (Balbix, Sentinel, etc.). Coverage gaps and agent health.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Software Inventory Count
goal_metric_definitions.description: Installed software per asset (CPE, vendor, product, version). SBOM and patch coverage.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Misconfiguration Count by Category
goal_metric_definitions.description: Misconfigurations per asset by category and severity. Config drift and compliance.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Software Patch Gap
goal_metric_definitions.description: Gap between latest available patch and installed patch. Patch lag and exposure.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Asset Open Ports Summary
goal_metric_definitions.description: Open ports per asset with service and traffic visibility. Attack surface and exposure.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Authenticated vs unauthenticated scan ratio
goal_metric_definitions.description: Compares counts and coverage of scans by TRACKING_METHOD (authenticated vs unauthenticated) per ASSET_ID/OS to identify gaps where authenticated scanning should be increased for deeper coverage.

goal_metric_definitions.name: CVE patch adoption latency
goal_metric_definitions.description: Measures time from CVE publication (or detection) to remediation for instances where a patch/fix_version exists, indicating how quickly patches are applied. Shorter adoption latency reduces risk exposure even when fixes are available.

goal_metric_definitions.name: CVE patch availability rate
goal_metric_definitions.description: Tracks the proportion of vulnerability instances that have a documented fix_version or patch entry to gauge remediation options availability. Knowing which instances lack patches helps prioritize workaround or mitigation actions.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Patch class risk concentration
goal_metric_definitions.description: Distribution and concentration of patch_class values across instances (e.g., critical/high/low) to identify vendors/products where high-risk patch classes are concentrated and require prioritized action.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Asset Inventory Count
goal_metric_definitions.description: Total count of assets (devices) by type, platform, site, and location. Coverage and inventory health.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Scheduled scan coverage & freshness
goal_metric_definitions.description: Measures the proportion of assets covered by scheduled scans and the time since last scheduled scan, showing how current scan scheduling keeps asset coverage fresh. This helps identify assets that are falling out of scanning cadence and therefore at higher undetected risk.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Vulnerability fix coverage by CVE and version
goal_metric_definitions.description: Tracks which vulnerabilities have an identified fixed_version or remediation guidance and the coverage of fixes across affected items. Important to know remediation availability and coverage to plan rollouts.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Vulnerabilities by Application
goal_metric_definitions.description: Identifies the number of vulnerabilities associated with each application version, allowing for targeted security improvements and risk assessments.

goal_metric_definitions.name: Asset centrality in attack paths
goal_metric_definitions.description: Counts how often an asset appears as a source or target across attack paths to identify hubs that concentrate risk and could amplify compromise. Central assets are good candidates for additional hardening or monitoring.

Produce a 30-day rolling series and compare the latest 30-day total to the prior 30-day total to show trend direction

planner_medallion_plan.reasoning:
Focus area 'vulnerabilities': The requested metrics require joins (host metadata + detections), time-series snapshots, consistent time-grain normalization, and derived fields (is_open, is_critical, days_open, numeric CVSS casting and high-CVSS flag). These require stable, materialized gold models per data source and per time grain so metrics can be computed by simple SELECTs against gold tables without rejoining silver tables. Following the medallion and source-separation rules, separate gold models are required for Qualys (host detections/hosts/applications) and Snyk (issues). Each time grain (daily/weekly/monthly) will be a separate gold model; weekly models will reference daily gold models, monthly will reference weekly models.

Focus area 'asset_inventory': The requested asset_inventory metrics require filtered time-series snapshots, joins between qualys silver tables (host detections, hosts, applications), and aggregations at daily/weekly/monthly grains (counts, trends, average days open, unique software counts). These are not simple one-table ad-hoc selects against a single silver table — they require: (a) stable daily snapshots that normalize snapshot_date and host context, (b) weekly and monthly aggregations for trends and reporting, and (c) application-level normalization for licensing and exposure metrics. Following the Medallion and source-separation rules, we will create qualys-source gold models only (the metrics are qualys-focused). We will create a hierarchical set of gold models per time grain (daily incremental snapshots built from qualys silver tables; weekly models that aggregate from daily gold models; monthly models that aggregate from weekly gold models). Each gold model is scoped to the asset_inventory focus area and to a single data source (qualys).

specifications.name: gold_qualys_vulnerabilities_daily_snapshot
specifications.description: Focus Area: vulnerabilities; Data Source: qualys; Time Grain: daily (foundation). Build from silver tables: qualys_host_detections, qualys_hosts, qualys_applications. Join qualys_host_detections.host_id = qualys_hosts.id to bring host attributes (ip, os). Left-join qualys_applications on host_id when application-level context is available (not required for provided metrics but included for lineage). Transformations: normalize a snapshot_date (DATE) from last_found_datetime (DATE_TRUNC day), derive is_open boolean from status (status values indicating open), derive is_critical boolean (severity indicates CRITICAL / or severity == '5' depending on the raw values), compute days_open = datediff(current_date, last_found_datetime) for quick backlog calculations, cast/normalize severity to canonical string. Output: a daily row-level snapshot of each detection with host metadata and derived fields. This model supports all Qualys-focused metrics by enabling simple aggregations (COUNT, AVG) and trends over snapshot_date without joining back to silver. How to compute each metric from columns in this model: - vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_host and vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_host_critical_vuln_count_per_host: SELECT host_id, ip, os, COUNT(*) AS critical_vuln_count WHERE is_critical = true AND is_open = true GROUP BY host_id, ip, os. - vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_host_30_day_trend_of_critical_vulnerabilities: use snapshot_date and is_critical/is_open to count per day over 30 days. - vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_os: GROUP BY os, severity or is_critical to compute counts, plus weekly trend by grouping snapshot_date by week. - vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:by_host and vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:trend_over_90_days: filter is_open = true; aggregate COUNT(*) by host_id and AVG(days_open) to compute backlog and average days open; use snapshot_date for 90-day trend.

specifications.description: Focus area: asset_inventory; data_source: qualys; time_grain: daily; entity: host_detections. Foundation daily incremental snapshot built from qualys_host_detections joined to qualys_hosts to attach host context. Use qualys_host_detections as the primary row-level detection source; left-join qualys_hosts on qualys_host_detections.host_id = qualys_hosts.id to bring ip/os/first_found_date. Normalize a snapshot_date = DATE_TRUNC('day', last_found_datetime) or model load date if last_found_datetime is null. Output is one row per detection per snapshot_date with host attributes and detection attributes so all vulnerability and backlog metrics (counts, trends, average-days calculations) can be computed by simple SELECTs (COUNT / AVG / GROUP BY) on this table without re-joining silver. Calculation guidance: critical vuln counts use severity='CRITICAL' and status IN (open statuses) and GROUP BY host_id or os; 30-day and 90-day trends use snapshot_date windows and counts of rows per snapshot_date; average days open is computed as AVG(DATEDIFF(day, last_found_datetime, snapshot_date)) for rows with status=open. This model supports metrics: vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_host, vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_host_critical_vuln_count_per_host, vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_host_30_day_trend_of_critical_vulnerabilities, vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_os, vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_os_critical_vuln_count_by_os, vuln_by_asset_criticality:by_os_weekly_trend_by_os, vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:by_host, vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:by_host_open_vulnerability_count_per_host, vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:by_host_average_days_open, vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:trend_over_90_days, vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:trend_over_90_days_open_vulnerability_trend_90d, vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:trend_over_90_days_change_in_backlog_size, asset_agent_coverage_hosts_without_recent_detections.

specifications.name: gold_qualys_host_detections_weekly_snapshot
specifications.description: Focus area: asset_inventory; data_source: qualys; time_grain: weekly; entity: host_detections. Weekly aggregation that references the daily snapshot for efficiency. Source: gold_qualys_host_detections_daily_snapshot. Aggregate daily rows into week (e.g., DATE_TRUNC('week', snapshot_date)) and produce weekly counts and summary fields per group (host, os, severity). Compute weekly open vulnerability counts per host/os, weekly average days open (AVG(days_since_last_found) for open items), and weekly snapshots for 7/30/90-day trend windows. This model is incremental (weekly) and optimized for trend dashboards. Metrics supported: weekly variants of vuln_by_asset_criticality (by_os_weekly_trend_by_os), vuln_fix_backlog_by_team:trend_over_90_days (roll-up), and host-level weekly counts.

source_columns.usage: filter open items for backlog and weekly aggregates
aggregate AVG(days_since_last_found) for weekly 'average days open'

specifications.name: gold_qualys_applications_weekly_snapshot
specifications.description: Focus area: asset_inventory; data_source: qualys; time_grain: weekly; entity: applications. Weekly aggregation referencing gold_qualys_applications_daily_snapshot. Aggregate unique installed software counts per host and per organization, detect 'new installs in last 7 days' by comparing snapshots within the 7-day window (daily-to-weekly rollups). Produce weekly unique_software_count per host/name/version and weekly application_count_per_host (proxy for exposure). Supports asset_software_licensing_compliance and asset_open_ports_summary metrics.
